header: 1. WAYS OF CLASSIFYING LIFE INSURANCE PRODUCTS
context: The document introduces the various ways life insurance products can be classified, starting with an overview of the four main categories: statutory insurance fund, product type, premium type, and ownership.
text:
<context>The document introduces the various ways life insurance products can be classified, starting with an overview of the four main categories: statutory insurance fund, product type, premium type, and ownership.</context>
# 1. WAYS OF CLASSIFYING LIFE INSURANCE PRODUCTS

1.1 There are many types of life insurance products available in the market. These products vary in many ways, such as their features and the benefits provided by them. Generally, they can be classified by way of:

 statutory insurance fund;   
product type; premium type; and/or ownership.

1.2 Let us look at each of the above classifications in turn.

context: This chunk is part of the "WAYS OF CLASSIFYING LIFE INSURANCE PRODUCTS" section, specifically detailing the classification by "STATUTORY INSURANCE FUND." It explains the regulatory requirement for separate insurance funds and lists the types of funds maintained for participating, non-participating, and investment-linked policies.
text:
<context>This chunk is part of the "WAYS OF CLASSIFYING LIFE INSURANCE PRODUCTS" section, specifically detailing the classification by "STATUTORY INSURANCE FUND." It explains the regulatory requirement for separate insurance funds and lists the types of funds maintained for participating, non-participating, and investment-linked policies.</context>
# CLASSIFICATION BY STATUTORY INSURANCE FUND

2.1 According to Section 16 of the Insurance Act 1966, insurers who are licensed to carry on insurance business are required to maintain insurance funds in respect of their insurance business. This is to ensure that the assets and liabilities of the shareholders and those relating to the insurance businesses are kept separate.

2.2 Life insurance companies generally establish and maintain separate insurance funds for the following types of life insurance policies:

Participating; Non-participating; and Investment-linked.

2.3 Participating and non-participating life insurance policies may be maintained in the same life insurance fund, but it is more common to have separate insurance funds, unless the volume of non-participating business is comparatively small. However, a separate insurance fund must be established and maintained for Investment-linked Life Insurance policies.

context: This chunk is part of the "WAYS OF CLASSIFYING LIFE INSURANCE PRODUCTS" section, specifically under the "CLASSIFICATION BY STATUTORY INSURANCE FUND" subheading, describing "Participating Policies".
text:
<context>This chunk is part of the "WAYS OF CLASSIFYING LIFE INSURANCE PRODUCTS" section, specifically under the "CLASSIFICATION BY STATUTORY INSURANCE FUND" subheading, describing "Participating Policies".</context>
# A. Participating Policies

2.4 Participating policies refer to life insurance products that share in the profits or surplus of the life insurance fund. Such products are also known as with-profits policies, and the policy owners receive from the insurer bonuses and / or dividends as declared from time to time.

context: This chunk is part of the "WAYS OF CLASSIFYING LIFE INSURANCE PRODUCTS" section, specifically under the "CLASSIFICATION BY STATUTORY INSURANCE FUND" subsection. It defines and explains non-participating policies, contrasting them with participating policies and providing examples.
text:
<context>This chunk is part of the "WAYS OF CLASSIFYING LIFE INSURANCE PRODUCTS" section, specifically under the "CLASSIFICATION BY STATUTORY INSURANCE FUND" subsection. It defines and explains non-participating policies, contrasting them with participating policies and providing examples.</context>
# B. Non-Participating Policies

2.5 Non-participating policies, on the other hand, refer to life insurance products that do not share in the profits or surplus of the insurance fund, i.e. the policy owners of such products are not entitled to any bonus or dividend payment by the insurer. Such products are also known as without-profits policies. Examples of non-participating policies include:

Universal Life Insurance policies; and Term Insurance policies.

Note: Whole Life and Endowment Insurance policies are offered in both participating and non-participating forms.

context: This chunk discusses Investment-linked Life Insurance Policies as a category of life insurance, specifically detailing their nature as investment products with an insurance component and how they differ from traditional policies. It falls under the broader section "WAYS OF CLASSIFYING LIFE INSURANCE PRODUCTS" and more specifically, under the sub-classification "CLASSIFICATION BY STATUTORY INSURANCE FUND".
text:
<context>This chunk discusses Investment-linked Life Insurance Policies as a category of life insurance, specifically detailing their nature as investment products with an insurance component and how they differ from traditional policies. It falls under the broader section "WAYS OF CLASSIFYING LIFE INSURANCE PRODUCTS" and more specifically, under the sub-classification "CLASSIFICATION BY STATUTORY INSURANCE FUND".</context>
# C. Investment-linked Life Insurance Policies

2.6 These are primarily investment products with an insurance protection element. They differ from the traditional life insurance products (i.e. Term, Whole Life and Endowment Insurance policies) in that they allow the policy owners to combine insurance protection with investment funds managed by the insurers or third-party fund managers.

2.7 The insurers are required to keep the assets and liabilities of the various life insurance funds separate from each other.

context: This chunk discusses the classification of life insurance products by their type, detailing the purpose of various products like Term, Whole Life, Endowment, Investment-linked, Universal Life, Annuities, Critical Illness, Long-Term Care, Medical Expense, and Disability Income insurance. It also begins to describe the specifics of health insurance types.
text:
<context>This chunk discusses the classification of life insurance products by their type, detailing the purpose of various products like Term, Whole Life, Endowment, Investment-linked, Universal Life, Annuities, Critical Illness, Long-Term Care, Medical Expense, and Disability Income insurance. It also begins to describe the specifics of health insurance types.</context>
# CLASSIFICATION BY PRODUCT TYPE

3.1 Life insurance products can also be classified according to product type, i.e. in accordance with the purpose served by the products. Table 3.1 shows the various products and the purposes served by them.

Table 3.1: Product Types And Their Purpose

table narrative: ["Term Insurance Policies provide death cover for a fixed term.", "Whole Life Insurance Policies provide death cover for the whole of the insured's life.", "Endowment Insurance Policies provide death cover for a fixed term and a lump sum at the end of the term.", "Investment-linked Life Insurance Policies (ILPs) provide mainly for investing in sub funds, unit trusts, or similar investments with insurance protection.", "Universal Life Insurance provides flexible death cover, premium, and premium payment period.", "Annuities provide retirement income.", "Critical Illness Insurance protects against contracting one of the covered critical illnesses.", "Long-Term Care Insurance protects against being unable to perform a specific number of Activities of Daily Living (ADLs), usually 3 out of 6 ADLs.", "Medical Expense Insurance protects against the risk of health and hospitalization.", "Disability Income Insurance protects against the risk of loss of income if a person is disabled."]

3.2 Critical Illness Insurance, Medical Expense Insurance, Disability Income Insurance and Long-Term Care Insurance are types of Health Insurance. We will briefly describe these products. For further details, do refer to the Health Insurance Study Text published by the SCI. The LIA’s publication: “Your Guide to Life Insurance” defines these health insurance products as the following:

3.3 Critical Illness Insurance - Will pay out a lump sum when the policy owner is diagnosed with a major illness. Some types of such policy have death cover as well. The illnesses that are covered vary, but usually include: heart attack of specified severity; stroke; coronary artery by-pass surgery; kidney failure; and major cancers.

3.4 Medical Expense Insurance – Basic Medical Expense Insurance, also known as Hospital and Surgical Insurance or Hospital Income Insurance, provides benefits for certain medical costs that result from accidents and sicknesses. Hospital and Surgical Insurance will reimburse the in-patient expenses that the policy owner has to pay while in hospital, depending on the limits in the policy. Hospital Income Insurance will pay a fixed daily amount of benefit based on the number of days that the policy owner is an in-patient. This will depend on the limits in the policy. Major medical insurance covers the expenses arising from a period longer than the limits in basic Hospital and Surgical Insurance.

3.5 Disability Income Insurance – This is an income-protection product, as it helps to replace part of the policy owner’s income if he becomes totally or partially disabled and cannot work as a result of an accident or sickness, under certain conditions.

3.6 Long-Term Care Insurance – Long-Term Care Insurance is designed to meet some or all of the costs of daily living of a person who, as a result of accident, sickness or old age, is physically disabled to the extent that he cannot

Whole Life Insurance Policies provide death cover for the whole of the insured's life.

an accident or sickness, under certain conditions.

3.6 Long-Term Care Insurance – Long-Term Care Insurance is designed to meet some or all of the costs of daily living of a person who, as a result of accident, sickness or old age, is physically disabled to the extent that he cannot take care of himself. He has to depend on others to help him carry out the most basic activities of daily living, such as feeding, bathing, moving around the home and so on.

3.7 For details of the other products in Table 3.1, do refer to subsequent chapters of this Study Text.

context: The document discusses ways of classifying life insurance products. Following classifications by statutory insurance fund, product type, and ownership, this section details the classification based on premium payment frequency.
text:
<context>The document discusses ways of classifying life insurance products. Following classifications by statutory insurance fund, product type, and ownership, this section details the classification based on premium payment frequency.</context>
# 4. CLASSIFICATION BY PREMIUM TYPE

4.1 Life insurance products can also be classified according to the frequency of premium payments. This includes:

Single Premium policy; Recurrent Single Premium policy; Regular Premium policy;  Yearly Renewable Premium policy; or Limited Premium Payment policy.

context: This section discusses the classification of life insurance policies based on who owns them, introducing categories like Single Life, Joint Life, and Third-party policies.
text:
<context>This section discusses the classification of life insurance policies based on who owns them, introducing categories like Single Life, Joint Life, and Third-party policies.</context>
# 5. CLASSIFICATION BY OWNERSHIP

5.1 Life insurance policies can be issued in a number of ways and can also be classified according to ownership of the policy. The different classifications are:

Single Life policy; Joint Life policy; Third-party policy; and

context: This chunk is part of the "CLASSIFICATION BY OWNERSHIP" section, specifically detailing different ways life insurance policies can be owned, with "Group policy" being the final category discussed under this classification.
text:
<context>This chunk is part of the "CLASSIFICATION BY OWNERSHIP" section, specifically detailing different ways life insurance policies can be owned, with "Group policy" being the final category discussed under this classification.</context>
# D. Group policy.

context: This chunk is part of the "CLASSIFICATION BY OWNERSHIP" section, specifically detailing the "Single Life Policy" as the most common type of life insurance.
text:
<context>This chunk is part of the "CLASSIFICATION BY OWNERSHIP" section, specifically detailing the "Single Life Policy" as the most common type of life insurance.</context>
# A. Single Life Policy

5.2 This is the most common type of life insurance policy issued, as most policies are taken on the proposer’s own life, i.e. the policy owner and the life insured are the same person. As the policy covers only one life, hence, the name of this policy is called Single Life policy.

context: This chunk discusses Joint Life Policies, a sub-classification of life insurance policies based on ownership, detailing "First-to-die" and "Last Survivor" types.
text:
<context>This chunk discusses Joint Life Policies, a sub-classification of life insurance policies based on ownership, detailing "First-to-die" and "Last Survivor" types.</context>
# B. Joint Life Policy

5.3 Generally, this policy provides cover for two lives (usually limited to only husband and wife). Note that, although the vast majority of joint life policies have two lives insured, it is theoretically possible to have more if insurable interest exists. It can be issued in either of the following ways:

First-to-die Life Insurance; or Last Survivor Life Insurance.

5.4 The First-to-die Life Insurance policy pays on the death of one of the lives insured. The death benefit is paid to the surviving life insured and the policy cover ends. An example of this type of policy is a Joint Mortgage Decreasing Term Insurance policy, usually taken at the request of the bank, as a collateral security for the housing loan which the bank has granted.

5.5 The Last Survivor Life Insurance (also known as Second-to-die Life Insurance) policy, on the other hand, only pays out the death benefit when both the lives insured have died i.e. on the second death. The premiums for this type of policy may be payable only until the first life insured dies or until the death of both lives insured. However, this type of policy is not common in Singapore.

context: This section defines a Third-Party Policy, a type of life insurance ownership, and contrasts it with Joint Life policies, explaining how one party may not be covered or how coverage can differ between the two individuals involved.
text:
<context>This section defines a Third-Party Policy, a type of life insurance ownership, and contrasts it with Joint Life policies, explaining how one party may not be covered or how coverage can differ between the two individuals involved.</context>
# C. Third-Party Policy

5.6 This policy also involves two persons. It is usually issued to the husband on the wife’s life and vice versa, or the parent on the child’s life. The differences between this and the joint life policy are that:

one of the parties may not be covered under the policy, e.g. the husband applies and owns the policy, but the insurance cover is on the wife’s life; and

 the cover for the two parties differs, e.g. a father purchases a $\$ 10,000$

Endowment Insurance policy on his child’s life and attaches a payor’s benefit rider to it. In this case, both the father and child are covered under the policy. Should anything happen

to the child, the sum assured of $\$ 10,000$ will be paid, but should anything happen to the father, only the future premiums due under the policy are waived until the child reaches a specified age (e.g. 21 years old).

context: This chunk is located in section 5, "CLASSIFICATION BY OWNERSHIP," specifically under subsection D, "Group policy." It introduces the concept of group policies as a type of life insurance ownership, differentiating them from individual policies and indicating that a more detailed discussion will follow in the next section.
text:
<context>This chunk is located in section 5, "CLASSIFICATION BY OWNERSHIP," specifically under subsection D, "Group policy." It introduces the concept of group policies as a type of life insurance ownership, differentiating them from individual policies and indicating that a more detailed discussion will follow in the next section.</context>
# D. Group Policy

As the name implies, this is a policy which covers a large number of lives. It is usually issued to employers covering the employees’ lives.

5.8 Group policies differ from individual policies in numerous ways and they also have certain advantages over individual policies. As it is important to understand these differences, some key aspects of Group Insurance are discussed in the next section.

context: This chunk is part of the section "CHARACTERISTICS OF GROUP LIFE INSURANCE", which details how group insurance operates, its key features like master contracts and minimal underwriting, and contrasts it with individual insurance.
text:
<context>This chunk is part of the section "CHARACTERISTICS OF GROUP LIFE INSURANCE", which details how group insurance operates, its key features like master contracts and minimal underwriting, and contrasts it with individual insurance.</context>
# 6. CHARACTERISTICS OF GROUP LIFE INSURANCE

6.1 Group Insurance provides coverage to many people under one group policy. The requirement is that several people must first be members of a group, before they become eligible to purchase the insurance. The group must have been formed for some purposes other than to obtain insurance. Examples are companies, trade unions, professional associations and social recreation clubs.

6.2 Characteristics of Group Insurance include:

(a) Master Contract – Group Insurance is issued under a single contract called the “master contract” which is kept by the policy owner. Unlike individual life insurance, when an insured member of the contract dies or leaves the group, only this member’s coverage is terminated. The plan itself will still carry on because it belongs to the holder of the master contract.

(b) Minimal Underwriting Requirements – It is generally made available to the participating employees (in active employment) with simpler medical underwriting if the group size is large. In some cases, the underwriter may require the employee to complete only a health declaration form. Medical examination is required only where the sum assured exceeds the “free cover limit”.

(c) Experience rating – It is usually underwritten based on the past claims experience if the insured group is considered to be large enough. Such a practice is called experience rating.

(d) Cost Effective – It provides low-cost protection resulting from savings in administrative costs, since only one policy is issued with a larger group of individuals being covered and less documentation involved. There is also a better spread of risk.

(e) Plan Continuation – The plan is usually renewable by the employer on a yearly basis. At the end of each policy period, your corporate client may wish to review the plan to assess the level of the coverage and its cost. You have to advise your client on this aspect.

6.3 There are quite a number of differences between Individual Insurance and Group Insurance. Table 3.2 shows the differences between them.

Table 3.2: Differences Between Individual Insurance And Group Insurance

table narrative: ["In Group Insurance, only members who belong to the group are covered, and their dependants may also be covered.", "In Group Insurance, only a master contract is issued for the group, although the number of insured persons, such as employees, is more than one.", "In Group Insurance, insured members may or may not have the right to decide on the amount of coverage they want to be covered for.", "In Group Insurance, for an employer-paid plan or compulsory plan, the sum assured is determined by the employer and can be a flat amount or a multiple of the employee's basic monthly salary, depending on the staff rank or category.", "In Group Insurance, the group as a whole is evaluated depending on the gender and age distribution of the group, and premiums are often subject to experience rating, especially when the group of insured lives is sizeable.", "In Group Insurance, coverage for the individual member stops when they leave the group.", "In Group Insurance, the plan continues for the remaining members, and the cost of coverage is lower because of group underwriting, where only limited evidence of insurability is usually required, and lower administrative costs and less documentation are involved."]

6.4 Since 1 October 2001, all representatives have been required to perform factfinding

narrative: In Group Insurance, only members who belong to the group are covered, and their dependants may also be covered.
In Group Insurance, only a master contract is issued for the group, although the number of insured persons, such as employees, is more than one.
In Group Insurance, insured members may or may not have the right to decide on the amount of coverage they want to be covered for.

In Group Insurance, for an employer-paid plan or compulsory plan, the sum assured is determined by the employer and can be a flat amount or a multiple of the employee's basic monthly salary, depending on the staff rank or category.

In Group Insurance, the group as a whole is evaluated depending on the gender and age distribution of the group, and premiums are often subject to experience rating, especially when the group of insured lives is sizeable.
In Group Insurance, coverage for the individual member stops when they leave the group.

In Group Insurance, the plan continues for the remaining members, and the cost of coverage is lower because of group underwriting, where only limited evidence of insurability is usually required, and lower administrative costs and less documentation are involved.

context: The document discusses ways to classify life insurance products, including by statutory insurance fund, product type, premium type, and ownership. Section 6 then delves into the characteristics of Group Life Insurance, detailing its features and contractual/voluntary plan structures. This chunk defines and differentiates between contractual (non-contributory) and voluntary (contributory) group insurance plans, setting the stage for a discussion of their respective advantages.
text:
<context>The document discusses ways to classify life insurance products, including by statutory insurance fund, product type, premium type, and ownership. Section 6 then delves into the characteristics of Group Life Insurance, detailing its features and contractual/voluntary plan structures. This chunk defines and differentiates between contractual (non-contributory) and voluntary (contributory) group insurance plans, setting the stage for a discussion of their respective advantages.</context>
# A. Contractual & Voluntary Plans

6.5 Group Insurance policies can be issued on a contractual or voluntary basis. For a contractual (non-contributory) plan, all the eligible employees will be contractually covered under the plan, and the premiums are paid by the employer. On the other hand, a voluntary (contributory) plan does not require full participation from the employees who are expected to pay part of the premiums. However, the insurer will normally require a minimum number of employees participating in the plan. The advantages of both types of plans are described below:

context: This section discusses the advantages of a contractual (non-contributory) group insurance plan, following the classification of group policies into contractual and voluntary types.
text:
<context>This section discusses the advantages of a contractual (non-contributory) group insurance plan, following the classification of group policies into contractual and voluntary types.</context>
# B. Advantages Of Contractual (Non-Contributory) Plan

(i) There is ease of administration, since there is no payroll deduction to monitor.   
(ii) There is lower cost owing to less administrative work involved and the greater pooling effect of risks as a result of many lives insured.   
(iii) The employer retains greater control in the benefit structures and coverage provisions.

context: This chunk discusses the advantages of a Voluntary (Contributory) Group Insurance Plan from both the employer's and employee's perspectives. It follows a description of Contractual (Non-Contributory) plans and precedes a comparison between Individual and Group Insurance.
text:
<context>This chunk discusses the advantages of a Voluntary (Contributory) Group Insurance Plan from both the employer's and employee's perspectives. It follows a description of Contractual (Non-Contributory) plans and precedes a comparison between Individual and Group Insurance.</context>
# C. Advantages Of Voluntary (Contributory) Plan

(i) Employer

Participating employees assume part of the costs of the benefits provided under the plan.

(ii) Employees

It gives the participating employees some control over the plan. They can obtain coverage at a lower premium rate as compared to that of buying it individually.

narrative: In Group Insurance, only members who belong to the group are covered, and members' dependants may also be covered, whereas in Individual Insurance, only the individual who has applied for the coverage is covered.

In Group Insurance, only a master contract is issued for the group, although the number of insured persons is more than one, whereas in Individual Insurance, each individual insured gets a policy contract.

In Group Insurance, insured members may or may not have the right to decide on the amount of coverage they want to be covered for, and in the employer-paid or compulsory plan, the sum assured is determined by the employer and can be a flat amount or a multiple of the employee's basic monthly salary, depending on the staff rank or category, whereas in Individual Insurance, the individual insured has the right to select the amount of coverage that they want to be covered for.

In Group Insurance, the group as a whole is evaluated depending on the gender and age distribution of the group, and premiums are often subject to experience rating, especially when the group of insured lives is sizeable, whereas in Individual Insurance, the individual's health and financial status are evaluated.

In Group Insurance, coverage for the individual member stops when they leave the group, but the plan continues for the remaining members, whereas in Individual Insurance, coverage continues until the individual chooses to terminate it before or upon policy expiry or maturity.

In Group Insurance, the cost of coverage is lower because of group underwriting, with only limited evidence of insurability usually required, and lower administrative costs and less documentation involved, whereas in Individual Insurance, the cost of coverage is higher because of individual underwriting and higher administrative costs involved.

context: This chunk summarizes key life insurance product types and their definitions, following a section on classifying life insurance products by statutory insurance fund and product type.
text:
<context>This chunk summarizes key life insurance product types and their definitions, following a section on classifying life insurance products by statutory insurance fund and product type.</context>
# Chapter summary:

- Definition or Explanation: Participating Policies
Life insurance products that share in the profits or surplus of the life insurance fund. Such products are also known as with-profits policies,and the policy ownersreceive from the insurer bonuses and / or dividends as declared from time to time.

- Definition or Explanation: Non-Participating Policies
Life insurance products that do not share in the profits or surplus of the insurance fund, i.e. the policy owners of such products are not entitled to any bonus or dividend payment by the insurer. Such products are also known as without-profitspolicies.

- Definition or Explanation: Investment-linked Life
These are primarily investment products with an insurance protection element.They differ from the traditional life insurance products (i.e. Term, Whole Life andEndowment Insurance policies) in that they aliow the policy owners to combine insurance protection with investment funds managed by the insurers or third-party fund managers.

- Definition or Explanation: Term Insurance Policies
Provide death cover for a fixed term.

- Definition or Explanation: Whole Life InsurancePolicies
Provide death cover for whole of the insured's life.

- Definition or Explanation: Endowment InsurancePolicies
Provide death cover for a fixed term and a lump sum at the end of the term.

- Definition or Explanation: Investment-linked Life
Provide mainly for investing in sub funds, unit trusts or similar investments withinsurance protection.

- Definition or Explanation: Universal Life Insurance
Provides flexible death cover, premium and premium payment period.

- Definition or Explanation: Annuities
Provide retirement income.

- Definition or Explanation: Critical Iliness Insurance
Protects against contracting one of the covered critical illnesses.

- Definition or Explanation: Long-Term Care Insurance
Protects against being unable to perform a specific number of Activities of Daily Living (ADLs)[usually 3 out 6 ADLs].

- Definition or Explanation: Medical Expense Insurance
Protects against risk of ill health and hospitalisation.

- Definition or Explanation: Disability IncomeInsurance
Protects against risk of loss of income if a person is disabled.

- Definition or Explanation: Single Life Policy
The most common type of life insurance policy issued, as most policies are taken on the proposer's own life,i.e. the policy owner and the

context: This chunk is an excerpt from a document detailing life insurance products. It focuses on the classification of these products and provides definitions and explanations for various policy types, including health insurance, individual life policies (single, joint, third-party), and group insurance plans (contractual and voluntary).
text:
<context>This chunk is an excerpt from a document detailing life insurance products. It focuses on the classification of these products and provides definitions and explanations for various policy types, including health insurance, individual life policies (single, joint, third-party), and group insurance plans (contractual and voluntary).</context>
hospitalisation.

- Definition or Explanation: Disability IncomeInsurance
Protects against risk of loss of income if a person is disabled.

- Definition or Explanation: Single Life Policy
The most common type of life insurance policy issued, as most policies are taken on the proposer's own life,i.e. the policy owner and the life insured are the same person. As the policy covers only one life, hence,the name of this policy is called Single Life policy.

- Definition or Explanation: Joint Life Policy
This policy provides cover for two lives (usually limited to only husband and wife).

- Definition or Explanation: First-to-die Life Insurancepolicy
The policy pays on the death of one of the lives insured.The death benefit is paid to the surviving life insured and the policy cover ends.

- Definition or Explanation: Last Survivor Life
Also known as Second-to-die Life Insurance policy. This policy only pays out thedeath benefit when both the lives insured have died i.e. on the second death. The premiums for this type of policy may be payable only until the first life insured dies or until the death of both lives insured.

- Definition or Explanation: Third-Party Policy
This policy also involves two persons. It is usually issued to the husband on thewife's life and vice versa, or the parent on the child's life.The difference between this policy and the joint life policy is that: one of the parties may not be covered under the policy the cover for the two parties differs

Diferences between contractual and voluntary plans   

# Chapter summary:

- Definition or Explanation: Group Policy
This is a policy which covers a large number of lives. It is usually issued to employers covering the employees' lives.

- Definition or Explanation: Contractual (non- contributory) plan
It is a type of Group Insurance policy where all the eligible employees will be contractually covered under the plan,and the premiums are paid by the employer. Advantages: Ease of administration Lower cost Employer retains greater control in the benefit structures and coverage provisions.

- Definition or Explanation: Voluntary (contributory) plan
It is a type of Group Insurance policy. It does not

text:

employees will be contractually covered under the plan,and the premiums are paid by the employer. Advantages: Ease of administration Lower cost Employer retains greater control in the benefit structures and coverage provisions.

- Definition or Explanation: Voluntary (contributory) plan
It is a type of Group Insurance policy. It does not require full participation from the employees who are expected to pay part of the premiums. However, the insurer will normally require a minimum number of employees participating in the plan. Advantages: For employers - Participating employees assume part of the costs of the benefits provided under the plan. For employees - Gives participating employees some control over the plan and can obtain coverage at a lower premium rate as compared to that of buying it
